summary:
Viral DNA integrates into specific areas within the liver cells' genetic material, particularly near genes responsible for regulating cell growth; this leads to mutations promoting tumor formation over time due to ongoing infection-induced damage and inflammatory responses. The mechanism involving integrated viral DNA stands out compared to merely high viral load or active infections alone because it directly impacts gene function crucial for preventing abnormal proliferation. To reiterate succinctly:

1. Hepatitis B can integrate its DNA into important parts of our liver’s own DNA.
2. Over years, this causes changes inside those special spots where they control when we grow new tissues—leading some people at higher risk getting sick livers later down the road even if feeling fine now. Summary: 
The integration of hepatitis B viral DNA into host chromosomes, especially around regulatory elements involved in controlling cell growth, triggers mutational processes contributing significantly to the development of hepatocellular carcinoma despite lack of symptoms initially. Chronic effects include persistent inflammation and genotoxic stress induced by continuous viral activity.
